what_it_does: Builds a modal layout allocation as a small AST invariant, sends the current device coordinate to a server-side resolver at /api/resolve, and writes the returned slot values to CSS custom properties on :root; on any failure it leaves the variables unset so existing CSS fallbacks remain effective.
observations: Uses local AST helpers (field, lit, mul, sub, clamp, select) that mirror a remote resolver grammar in rules.py.
MODAL_INVARIANT expresses continuous and discrete layout relationships (pad_top, pad_x, frame_max_h) rather than hard-coded breakpoint values.
Posts {invariant, coordinate} to /api/resolve and expects {ok, resolved:{slot:value}}.
Applies resolved values to CSS vars --res-modal-pad-top, --res-modal-pad-x, --res-modal-frame-max-h, appending px for numeric slots.
Includes a monotonic sequence guard so stale async resolves from older coordinates cannot overwrite newer ones during resize storms.
Explicitly degrades cleanly: any HTTP, parse, or contract failure leaves CSS vars unset, preserving body[data-ff] fallbacks.
Comments describe a deployment timing issue where /api/resolve may be committed-not-live until a bridge bounce lands.
standouts: The invariant is authored as an AST sent to a remote resolver rather than evaluated locally with CSS media queries or JS math.
Heavy inline contract commentary tying every behavior to RESOLVER-CONTRACT.md sections and a known stale-bridge failure mode.
Primitive-type guard prevents an AST object from being stringified as [object Object] into a CSS value.
conventions: TypeScript with explicit unknown typing for AST nodes.
CSS custom properties used as the integration surface between JS resolver consumer and stylesheet.
Async fetch with try/catch and non-throwing degrade-clean behavior.
Comments as design documentation referencing external contract documents.
concerns: The remote resolver grammar is only implied by local AST helpers; mismatch between this file and the live rules.py would silently degrade or produce wrong values.
PX_SLOTS is a client-side set that must stay synchronized with which slots the resolver treats as pixel dimensions.
Resolved values are written directly to CSS without further range validation beyond the resolver's own clamp.
If /api/resolve is stale or returns a teaching error, the file intentionally does nothing, which is correct per contract but could mask resolver bugs until a bounce.
notes: This is the host-side consumer of a server-side layout resolver: it ships an AST invariant and a device coordinate, then maps the response into CSS variables, with careful failure fallback.
questions: What is the exact grammar and operator set supported by /api/resolve in rules.py?
How is the resolver contract versioned so that MODAL_INVARIANT stays compatible with stale and future bridge bounces?
Where are the CSS rules that consume --res-modal-* variables and provide the body[data-ff] fallbacks?
Is there a reason pad_x defaults to 12 for unknown orientations while the landscape case is also 12?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose (host half of resolve(device-coordinate)->allocation, degrade-clean, bounce-ready) matches the implementation: it builds the invariant, calls /api/resolve, and applies or skips CSS vars exactly as described.
intention_signals: Move modal layout decisions out of the client and into a server-side resolver engine while keeping the surface visually stable during resolver rollout or network failures.
novelty: The invariant-as-AST pattern: layout relationships are serialized and evaluated remotely, with local CSS acting only as the final value sink.
contribution: A self-contained consumer that defines the modal allocation invariant, obtains resolver output for the current window coordinate, and surfaces the results through CSS variables, including guards and fallback behavior that keep the UI unchanged when the resolver is unavailable.
summary_for_embedding: resolveAllocation.ts is the host-side consumer of a server-side layout resolver that posts a modal allocation AST invariant plus the current device coordinate to /api/resolve and writes the returned slot values to CSS custom properties, falling back to existing CSS when the resolver fails.
intention_for_embedding: To let a remote resolver compute modal layout allocations from screen coordinates while keeping the surface visually stable during resolver unavailability or async races.

symbols.does: AST builder for a field reference.
symbols.description: Returns an object with op 'field' and the given path, representing a value read from the coordinate object by the remote resolver.

symbols.does: AST builder for a literal value.
symbols.description: Returns an object with op 'lit' and the supplied primitive value, used to embed constants in the invariant.
Returns an object with op 'mul' and the two operands, expressing a product in the resolver AST.
Returns an object with op 'sub' and the two operands, expressing a difference in the resolver AST.

symbols.does: AST builder for a clamp operation.
symbols.description: Returns an object with op 'clamp' and the value plus lower/upper bounds, used to keep a computed value within a range.

symbols.does: AST builder for a conditional selection.
symbols.description: Returns a select object with a field path, case mapping, and optional default, letting the resolver choose a value based on a discrete field such as orientation.

symbols.does: Type alias for the device coordinate.
symbols.description: Declares the shape of the coordinate object passed to the resolver, containing device width, height, and orientation.

symbols.does: Captures the current viewport as a Coordinate.
symbols.description: Reads window.innerWidth and window.innerHeight, derives portrait/landscape from h > w, and returns the coordinate object used by the resolver.

symbols.does: Resolves modal allocation and applies it to CSS vars.
symbols.description: Posts MODAL_INVARIANT and the current coordinate to /api/resolve, guards against stale async results with a sequence counter, validates that resolved slot values are primitives, and writes them to :root CSS custom properties with px units where appropriate; on any failure it removes the variables so CSS fallbacks remain active.
